analogy: Think of "Cell The Unit of Life" through the chapter's own opening images: UNIT 3 Biology is the study of living organisms. The detailed description of their form and appearance only brought out their diversity. It is the cell theory that emphasised the unity underlying this diversity of forms, i.e., the cellular organisation of all life forms. A... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CELL: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS, 8.1 WHAT IS A CELL?, 8.2 CELL THEORY, CELL: THE UNIT OF LIFE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Cell The Unit of Life is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that uNIT 3 Biology is the study of living organisms.

The text explains that the detailed description of their form and appearance only brought out their diversity.

The text explains that it is the cell theory that emphasised the unity underlying this diversity of forms, i.e., the cellular organisation of all life forms.

The text explains that a description of cell structure and cell growth by division is given in the chapters comprising this unit.

The text explains that cell theory also created a sense of mystery around living phenomena, i.e., physiological and behavioural processes.

The text explains that this mystery was the requirement of integrity of cellular organisation for living phenomena to be demonstrated or observed.

The text explains that in studying and understanding the physiological and behavioural processes, one can take a physico-chemical approach and use cell-free systems to investigate.

The text explains that this approach enables us to describe the various processes in molecular terms.

The text explains that the approach is established by analysis of living tissues for elements and compounds.

The text explains that it will tell us what types of organic compounds are present in living organisms.

The text explains that in the next stage, one can ask the question: What are these compounds doing inside a cell?

The text explains that and, in what way they carry out gross physiological processes like digestion, excretion, memory, defense, recognition, etc.

The text explains that in other words we answer the question, what is the molecular basis of all physiological processes?

The text explains that it can also explain the abnormal processes that occur during any diseased condition.

The text explains that this physico-chemical approach to study and understand living organisms is called ‘Reductionist Biology’.

The text explains that the concepts and techniques of physics and chemistry are applied to understand biology.

The text explains that in Chapter 9 of this unit, a brief description of biomolecules is provided.

The text explains that cELL: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS Chapter 8 Cell: The Unit of Life Chapter 9 Biomolecules Chapter 10 Cell Cycle and Cell Division

The text explains that rAMACHANDRAN, an outstanding figure in the field of protein structure, was the founder of the ‘Madras school’ of conformational analysis of biopolymers.

The text explains that his discovery of the triple helical structure of collagen published in Nature in 1954 and his analysis of the allowed conformations of proteins through the use of the ‘Ramachandran plot’ rank among the most outstanding contributions in structural biology.

The text explains that while at Cambridge, Ramachandran met Linus Pauling and was deeply influenced by his publications on models of the α-helix and β-sheet structures that directed his attention to solving the structure of collagen.

The text explains that anything less than a complete structure of a cell does not ensure independent living.

The text explains that at about the same time, Theodore CELL: THE UNIT OF LIFE CHAPTER 8 8.1 What is a Cell?

The text explains that this theory however, did not explain as to how new cells were formed.

The text explains that rudolf Virchow (1855) first explained that cells divided and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula-e cellula).

The text explains that the cells of the human cheek have an outer membrane as the delimiting structure of the cell.

The text explains that inside each cell is a dense membrane bound structure called nucleus.

The text explains that besides the nucleus, the eukaryotic cells have other membrane bound distinct structures called organelles like the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), the golgi complex, lysosomes, mitochondria, microbodies and vacuoles.

The text explains that the cell envelope consists of a tightly bound three layered structure i.e., the outermost glycocalyx followed by the cell wall and then the plasma membrane.

The text explains that a special membranous structure is the mesosome which is formed by the extensions of plasma membrane into the cell.

The text explains that they also help in respiration, secretion processes, to increase the surface area of the plasma membrane and enzymatic content.

The text explains that besides flagella, Pili and Fimbriae are also surface structures of the bacteria but do not play a role in motility.

The text explains that the pili are elongated tubular structures made of a special protein.

The text explains that in some bacteria, they are known to help attach the bacteria to rocks in streams and also to the host tissues.

The text explains that these are not bound by any membrane system and lie free in the cytoplasm, e.g., phosphate granules, cyanophycean granules and glycogen granules.

The text explains that in addition, eukaryotic cells have a variety of complex locomotory and cytoskeletal structures.

The text explains that cELL: THE UNIT OF LIFE 93 Cholesterol Sugar Peripheral Protein Phospholipid bilayer Let us now look at individual cell organelles to understand their structure and functions.

The text explains that 8.5.1 Cell Membrane The detailed structure of the membrane was studied only after the advent of the electron microscope in the 1950s.

The text explains that meanwhile, chemical studies on the cell membrane, especially in human red blood cells (RBCs), enabled the scientists to deduce the possible structure of plasma membrane.

The text explains that 94 BIOLOGY An improved model of the structure of cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972) widely accepted as fluid mosaic model (Figure 8.4).

The text explains that neutral solutes may move across the membrane by the process of simple diffusion along the concentration gradient, i.e., from higher concentration to the lower.

The text explains that such a transport is an energy dependent process, in which ATP is utilised and is called active transport, e.g., Na+/K+ Pump.

The text explains that 8.5.2 Cell Wall As you may recall, a non-living rigid structure called the cell wall forms an outer covering for the plasma membrane of fungi and plants.

The text explains that algae have cell wall, made of cellulose, galactans, mannans and minerals like calcium carbonate, while in other plants it consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, pectins and proteins.

The text explains that if not, then how are these transported across the membrane?

The text explains that name two cell-organelles that are double membrane bound.

The text explains that what are the characteristics of these two organelles?

The text explains that state their functions and draw labelled diagrams of both.

The text explains that what are the characteristics of prokaryotic cells?

The text explains that multicellular organisms have division of labour.

The text explains that both lysosomes and vacuoles are endomembrane structures, yet they differ in terms of their functions.

The text explains that describe the structure of the following with the help of labelled diagrams.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8: Cell The Unit of Life

schemes.point: It is the cell theory that emphasised the unity underlying this diversity of forms, i.e., the cellular organisation of all life forms. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch8 pg 1)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The organisation of the prokaryotic cell is fundamentally similar even though prokaryotes exhibit a wide variety of shapes and functions. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch8 pg 4)

schemes.name: Although
schemes.point: Although each layer of the envelope performs distinct function, they act together as a single protective unit. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch8 pg 5)

schemes.name: Both
schemes.point: Both the centrioles in a centrosome lie perpendicular to each other in which each has an organisation like the cartwheel. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch8 pg 14)

prelims_traps: Do not treat CELL: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS and 8.1 WHAT IS A CELL? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Cell The Unit of Life; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Cell The Unit of Life through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CELL: STRUCTURE AND FUNCTIONS and 8.1 WHAT IS A CELL? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
